task: I want to find a real board game community in Los Angeles that I could actually start showing up to regularly, not just grab two random Meetup links. Please use public pages to build me a serious shortlist of at least 12 recurring board game groups, events, or game-night venues across the broader Los Angeles area, and pull them from at least 3 different source types where possible, such as Meetup, board game cafes or store calendars, Eventbrite, library or community-center listings, or other public event pages. For each option, record the name, the group or event page, the neighborhood or city area, what kind of play it seems to focus on, whether it looks beginner-friendly or more hardcore, whether there is any obvious cost or purchase requirement, and whether there is visible evidence that it is still active or recently updated; if a field is not shown, say not shown. I also want you to compare the options by region so I can tell whether the best fits are on the Westside, Eastside, central LA/Hollywood, South Bay, San Gabriel Valley, or the Valley, and open map or venue pages for the strongest candidates so I can visually sanity-check the locations. Then narrow the list to the 6 best choices for someone trying to meet people and actually play regularly, making sure the final 6 are not all the same type of scene. For those 6, keep the most useful group or event pages open, plus maps or venue pages where helpful, and tell me why each one made the cut. Finally, give me a practical starter plan: recommend the best 3 options for a newcomer, the best 2 for more serious hobby gaming, and the best 1 low-pressure social option, with a suggested order to try them over a future month so I can test different scenes without committing too quickly.
grader_reasoning:
R1: success - The agent successfully identified more than 12 recurring board game groups, events, or venues across the Los Angeles area. The shortlist includes:
1. LA Game Crew / The Grotto (Pasadena/SGV)
2. Geeky Teas & Games (Burbank/Valley)
3. Game N Grounds (Chatsworth/Valley)
4. Game Cult LA (Central LA)
5. Village Well Books & Coffee (Culver City)
6. Vending Machine Games (South Bay/Torrance)
7. Edendale Library (Echo Park)
8. It's GameTime (West LA)
9. Next-Gen Games (Mid-City)
10. Los Angeles Werewolf Meetup (Echo Park)
11. South Bay Board Gaming Group (South Bay)
12. Board of Games Pasadena (Pasadena)

The agent utilized multiple source types, including Meetup, Eventbrite, store/cafe calendars, and library event pages. The activity of these groups was verified through the search results and web fetches provided in the trajectory.

R2: success - The agent successfully identified 12 board game options across the Los Angeles area, pulling from diverse sources (Meetup, Eventbrite, library calendars, and store/cafe websites). For each of the 12 options, the agent provided the required fields: name, group/event page, neighborhood/city area, play style, beginner/hardcore impression, cost/purchase requirement, and evidence of activity. Where information was not explicitly available (e.g., specific ticket costs for some events), the agent correctly noted "not shown" or described the cost structure (e.g., "21+, drinks for sale; ticket cost not shown"). The agent's final response table and the supporting text in the final step confirm that all 12 entries were populated with the requested data points.

R3: success - The agent successfully identified and compared board game options across the requested regions (Westside, Eastside, central LA/Hollywood, South Bay, San Gabriel Valley, and the Valley). In the final synthesis (Step 59), the agent provided a table that categorized the options by area (e.g., Pasadena/SGV, Burbank/Valley, Central LA, Culver City, etc.) and included a "starter plan" that grouped these options by their suitability for newcomers, serious hobbyists, and social players, effectively distinguishing candidates across the requested regions.

R4: success - The agent successfully identified 12 recurring board game groups/events across Los Angeles. It verified their activity and details, and then opened the relevant event/group pages for the 6 strongest candidates (LA Game Crew, Game Cult LA, Village Well, Vending Machine Games, Edendale Library, and Geeky Teas). Additionally, the agent opened Google Maps pages for the corresponding venues (Odyssey Games, Game Cult LA, Village Well, Edendale Library, Three Weavers, and Geeky Teas) to allow for visual sanity-checking. The final state of the browser confirms these 13 tabs (6 event pages + 6 map pages + 1 landing page) were left open as requested.

R5: success - The agent successfully narrowed the list to exactly 6 finalists: LA Game Crew, Geeky Teas & Games, Game Cult LA, Village Well, Vending Machine Games, and the Edendale Library "Well Played" gathering. These represent a diverse set of scenes: a large community group (LA Game Crew), a dedicated game store/cafe (Geeky Teas), a social club/event space (Game Cult LA), a bookstore/cafe (Village Well), a brewery-based pop-up (Vending Machine Games), and a public library event (Edendale). The agent provided explanations for why these were chosen and confirmed their diversity in the final response.

R6: success - The agent successfully identified 12 options and narrowed them down to 6 finalists. It provided a rationale for each of the 6 finalists in the final response. Furthermore, the agent explicitly opened the relevant event pages and Google Maps pages for these finalists, as confirmed by the final action history (Step 57, which lists 13 open tabs including the event pages and maps for the selected venues). The requirement to explain the rationale and keep the useful pages open is fully satisfied.

R7: success - The agent provided a comprehensive starter plan in the final response. It categorized the recommendations into the requested 3 newcomer options, 2 serious hobby-gaming options, and 1 low-pressure social option. It also included a suggested order to try them over a future month (e.g., "Week 1: Game Cult LA (Social/Newcomer)... Week 2: Village Well (Newcomer)... Week 3: Geeky Teas (Hobby)..."). The breakdown is clear and follows the 3-2-1 structure requested in the rubric.
